programs.name: TennCare Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)

income_limits.description: Income limits are based on Federal Poverty Level (FPL) and vary by program and household composition. 2026 limits are:
income_limits.note: Income limits increase annually in January. Applicants should verify current year limits with TennCare.

asset_limits.what_counts: Money in bank accounts, stocks, and bonds
asset_limits.what_does_not_count: Primary residence, one car, burial plot, up to $1,500 in burial expenses, furniture, household items, and personal items

other_requirements: Must be entitled to Medicare Part A (with limited exceptions for QI-1 for individuals under 65 who lost Part A entitlement due to returning to work)
Must have both Part A and Part B to qualify for SLMB or QI programs
Cannot be enrolled in TennCare Medicaid or TennCare Standard to qualify for QI-1
If eligible for Medicaid, cannot receive QI benefits but may qualify for another MSP
QI-1 recipients must reapply every year; applications granted on first-come, first-served basis with priority given to prior-year recipients

covers: Medicare Part A premiums (for individuals without premium-free Part A)
Full extra help for Part D plans

SLMB.note: Does not provide full Medicaid benefits.

Full extra help for Part D plans
QI_1.note: Does not provide full Medicaid benefits. Offered on first-come, first-served basis until funds run out.

gotchas: QI-1 is NOT available to CHOICES recipients[5]
QI-1 operates on first-come, first-served basis with limited funding; applications may be denied once funds are exhausted for the year[3][4]
QI-1 requires annual reapplication; benefits are not automatic renewal[4]

programs.data_shape_notes: This program consists of three distinct tiers (QMB, SLMB, QI-1) with progressively higher income thresholds but decreasing benefit comprehensiveness. QMB is mandatory for states; SLMB and QI-1 are optional but Tennessee offers them. QI-1 has unique constraints: first-come, first-served funding model, annual reapplication requirement, and exclusion for CHOICES recipients and those already on TennCare Medicaid. Income and asset limits are indexed to Federal Poverty Level and change annually. Specific application procedures, processing timelines, required forms, and regional office locations are not detailed in available search results and require direct contact with TennCare.

programs.name: Tennessee SNAP

eligibility.income_limits: For households with any member 60 or older or disabled: no gross income limit, but must meet net income test (net income calculated as gross minus deductions like 20% earned income deduction, standard deduction ($209 for 1-3 people), shelter/utility up to $744, medical over $35 for elderly/disabled). General households: gross income ≤130% FPL (e.g., Oct 2025-2026: ~$15,060/year or $1,255/month for 1 person; $20,440/year or $1,703/month for 2 people; exact table varies by size—use official calculator). Seniors 60+ exempt from gross income test[1][3][6][7].
eligibility.asset_limits: Households with member 60+ or disabled are exempt from asset test. General households subject to asset test (limits not specified in sources; most retirement accounts like 401Ks exempt, home and one car typically exempt)[1][8].
other_requirements: Tennessee resident[6].
U.S. citizen or qualified non-citizen.

Seniors 60+ and disabled exempt from work requirements (ABAWD rules apply to able-bodied adults 18-under 65 without dependents/children under 14; must work/volunteer/train 80 hours/month or limited to 3 months benefits in 3 years)[2][4][5][6][9].
Household defined as those who buy/prepare food together; parents/children ≤21 considered one[6].

benefits.value: Monthly EBT card for food purchases (amount based on net income, household size, deductions; e.g., example 2-person elderly household: $415/month after 30% net income subtraction from max allotment of $546). Excludes sugary foods in TN[2]. Max allotments vary (e.g., Table 1 federal for Oct 2025-26 not fully detailed here)[7].

methods: Online: tn.gov/humanservices (via DHS portal)[6][10].
Phone: Contact local TDHS office (TDHS helpline not specified; use 1-866-311-4287 or local via directory)[10].
Mail/In-person: Local TDHS county offices (find via tn.gov/humanservices).[10].
application.processing_time: Not specified in sources; typically 30 days standard, expedited for urgent cases.
forms: Online application via TN.gov DHS portal; no specific form number listed[6][10].
documents: Proof of income (Social Security, pension, pay stubs—all count)[3][7].
ID/residency proof.
Medical expenses over $35/month for 60+/disabled.
Rent/mortgage, utility bills for deductions.
Proof of disability if claiming.

geography.regional_variations: Administered uniformly by TDHS via local county offices; no major differences noted, but apply via local office; some food banks offer volunteer ops for ABAWD[5].
offices_or_providers: Local TDHS county offices statewide (directory at tn.gov/humanservices)[10].

gotchas: Seniors 60+ have no gross income limit but must pass net income test—many miss deductions like medical >$35, shelter up to $744, boosting eligibility[1][6][7].
All income counts (SS, VA, pensions); household includes food-sharers[3][6].
Recent 2025 ABAWD changes extend work rules to under 65 (exempts 65+ and caregivers)[2][5][9].
No asset test for elderly/disabled, but verify exemptions (home/car ok)[8].
Utility allowances inflation-adjusted; check current[1].
programs.data_shape_notes: Elderly/disabled households exempt from gross income and asset tests, net only; benefits calculated via 30% net income rule from max allotment; scales by household size; statewide with local offices.

programs.name: Tennessee LIHEAP

eligibility.income_limits: Income eligibility varies by source and year: at or below 150% of U.S. Poverty Guidelines (e.g., older data: 1-person $17,505/year; 2 $23,595; 3 $29,685; 4 $35,775; 5 $41,865; 6 $47,955) or 60% of State Median Income (check current chart at local agency). Recent monthly gross income limits: 1-person $2,671; 2 $3,492; 3 $4,314; 4 $5,136; 5 $5,958; 6 $6,780. Automatic eligibility if receiving SNAP, SSI, TANF, or certain Veterans benefits.[1][2][4][5]
other_requirements: Must meet both income eligibility and priority points (targets elderly, disabled, households with children under 6); live in service area of local agency; anyone can apply but prioritized based on need (e.g., shut-off, emergency).[2][3]

benefits.value: One-time payment to utility company for heating/cooling: Regular $174-$750 (varies by energy burden); max heating/cooling $1,000, min $600; crisis max $1,000; older summer cooling max $325, heating/emergency $250. Available once per year from up to two of three programs (regular, crisis, summer).[1][4][5]

methods: Online via SmartSimple starting November 1, 2025 at 8:00am CST (thda.org link); contact local agency for phone/in-person/mail (e.g., ETHRA crisis: Anderson 865-691-2551, Campbell 423-562-2948, etc.); find local agency at THDA LIHEAP contacts PDF.[3][4]
application.processing_time: Not specified; placed on waitlist until funds available.
application.waitlist: Yes, eligible applicants waitlisted until federal funds released (e.g., 2026 funding pending); crisis prioritized.[3]
forms: SmartSimple online application; additional docs via THDA application documents link.[4]
documents: Proof of income, household size, utility bills/shut-off notices (check local agency for full list).[4]

geography.restrictions: All 95 counties via 19-20 local non-profit/government agencies.
geography.regional_variations: Administered by local agencies (e.g., ETHRA for Anderson, Campbell, Claiborne, Morgan, Scott, Union counties); applications, wait times, assistance vary by agency and funding; contact local provider.[3][4][5]
offices_or_providers: Network of 19 local agencies; THDA contacts PDF for specifics.[4][5]

gotchas: Funding not guaranteed (e.g., federal delays cause waitlists/no awards); must meet both income AND priority points; benefits one-time only; older vs. current income limits conflict (verify with local agency); crisis prioritized over regular; applies Nov-May typically.[1][2][3][4]
programs.data_shape_notes: Administered statewide via 19 regional agencies with varying providers, waitlists, and benefit calculations by energy burden/household; priority points system beyond income; federal funding delays common; income at 60% SMI or 150% FPG per source.

## Key Guidance for Families with Elderly Loved Ones

**Income is the primary barrier.** Your elderly relative must have household income at or below 200% of federal poverty guidelines[2][3]. For a single person in 2026, verify the current income limit with your local provider—it's indexed annually[2].

**Automatic qualification exists.** If your elderly relative receives SSI or TANF, they automatically qualify without an income test[2].

**Age 60+ gets priority, not preference.** There is no minimum age requirement, but households with someone over 60 receive priority service[2]. This means they move up in the queue but doesn't guarantee faster processing.

**You must find your local provider.** THDA doesn't directly serve applicants—you must contact the weatherization agency in your county using THDA's Weatherization Agency Locator[7]. This is critical because application methods, timelines, and contact information vary by county[8].

**Services are customized, not standardized.** After a certified energy auditor assesses the home, the program provides specific weatherization measures (insulation, air sealing, window replacement, etc.) based on that audit[7]. There are no dollar caps or service limits specified in program documentation.

**Processing time is unknown.** Search results do not specify how long applications take or whether waitlists exist in your county[7]. Contact your local provider directly for this information.

**Documentation is straightforward.** Bring proof of identity, citizenship documentation, proof of Tennessee residence, and income records for the past 12 months[5].

programs.name: Tennessee State Health Insurance Assistance Program (TN SHIP)

eligibility.age: 65 or older, OR any age if on Medicare due to disability[6]
eligibility.income_limits: Not specified in available sources. The program serves 'people with limited incomes'[2] but specific dollar thresholds are not provided in search results.
other_requirements: Must be Medicare-eligible[6]
Anyone with questions about healthcare benefits can reach out, but primary focus is Medicare-eligible individuals[6]

benefits.value: Free, unbiased one-on-one counseling and education; no direct financial payments from this program, though counselors help beneficiaries apply for programs that DO pay (Medicare Savings Plans, Extra Help/Low Income Subsidy, Medicaid)[2][4]
specific_services: One-on-one assistance reviewing Medicare health or prescription drug plan options[6]
Eligibility determination for Medicare health or prescription drug plans[6]
Counseling on Medicare Part A, Part B, Part D, Medicare Advantage (HMOs/PPOs), Medigap, and Medicare Supplement Insurance[1][5]
Application assistance for Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB, QI)[1][5]
Application assistance for Extra Help/Low Income Subsidy programs[2][5]
Medicaid and other insurance program information[1][5]
Prescription drug assistance programs and discount cards[5]
Medicare billing error reporting and fraud detection through Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP)[2]
Community outreach at health fairs and senior events[2][3]
benefits.varies_by: not_applicable — counseling is free regardless of income or household size, though eligibility for programs SHIP helps with (like Medicare Savings Plans) may vary

methods: Phone: 1-877-801-0044 (toll-free statewide)[3][4][8]
Email: dda.ship@tn.gov[4]
Online form: Available on state website[4]
In-person: Through nine regional SHIP offices across Tennessee[3]
Text: (931) 345-3635 (Upper Cumberland region)[7]
Local phone numbers vary by region (e.g., Shelby County: 901-222-4111)[3]
application.processing_time: Not specified in available sources. Described as 'local, in-depth' counseling but no timeline provided.
application.forms: No specific form names or numbers provided in search results. Online form available but not named.
application.documents: Not specified in available sources. No documentation requirements listed.

geography.restrictions: Program operates statewide through nine regional SHIP offices[3]
geography.regional_variations: Yes — different regional offices serve different counties. Examples: Aging Commission of the Mid-South serves Shelby, Fayette, Lauderdale, and Tipton counties[3]; Upper Cumberland SHIP serves 14-county Upper Cumberland region[7]
offices_or_providers: Nine regional SHIP offices statewide[3]
Aging Commission of the Mid-South (Shelby, Fayette, Lauderdale, Tipton counties): 901-222-4111 or 877-801-0044[3]
Upper Cumberland SHIP (14-county region): (931) 432-4150 or (931) 345-3635[7]
Statewide coordination: Tennessee Commission on Aging and Disability[3]

gotchas: This is NOT a financial assistance program — it provides counseling only. Families expecting direct payment for premiums should know SHIP helps you APPLY for programs that pay (Medicare Savings Plans, Extra Help), but SHIP itself doesn't pay[2][4]
SHIP does not sell insurance or endorse any insurance company — it is unbiased, which is valuable but means it won't recommend a specific plan[9]
If someone is on an ACA Marketplace plan when they become Medicare-eligible, they MUST disenroll from the Marketplace plan or face penalties — SHIP can help with this transition[4]
Medicare Open Enrollment Period ends December 7th annually — timing matters for plan changes[4]
Translation services are available in multiple languages at some regional offices[7]
The program also offers Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP) services to help detect and report Medicare fraud — this is a separate but related service[2][9]
Volunteer opportunities exist if families want to help others while learning the program[4][6]
No income or asset limits are specified in public materials — eligibility appears to be based solely on Medicare status and age/disability, not means-testing
programs.data_shape_notes: TN SHIP is a counseling and advocacy program, not a financial assistance program. It operates through nine regional offices with local phone numbers but a unified statewide toll-free line. The program's primary value is helping beneficiaries navigate complex Medicare options and apply for separate income-based programs (Medicare Savings Plans, Extra Help). No income limits, asset limits, or processing times are publicly specified. Eligibility is straightforward: Medicare-eligible individuals age 65+ or any age if on Medicare due to disability. The program is federally funded but administered by the Tennessee Commission on Aging and Disability.

```json
    "SHIP does not sell insurance or endorse any insurance company — it is unbiased, which is valuable but means it won't recommend a specific plan[9]",
    "If someone is on an ACA Marketplace plan when they become Medicare-eligible, they MUST disenroll from the Marketplace plan or face penalties — SHIP can help with this transition[4]",
    "Medicare Open Enrollment Period ends December 7th annually — timing matters for plan changes[4]",
    "Translation services are available in multiple languages at some regional offices[7]",
    "The program also offers Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP) services to help detect and report Medicare fraud — this is a separate but related service[2][9]",
    "Volunteer opportunities exist if families want to help others while learning the program[4][6]",
    "No income or asset limits are specified in public materials — eligibility appears to be based solely on Medicare status and age/disability, not means-testing"
  ],
  "data_shape_notes": "TN SHIP is a counseling and advocacy program, not a financial assistance program. It operates through nine regional offices with local phone numbers but a unified statewide toll-free line. The program's primary value is helping beneficiaries navigate complex Medicare options and apply for separate income-based programs (Medicare Savings Plans, Extra Help). No income limits, asset limits, or processing times are publicly specified. Eligibility is straightforward: Medicare-eligible individuals age 65+ or any age if on Medicare due to disability. The program is federally funded but administered by the Tennessee Commission on Aging and Disability."
}
```

## Key Takeaways for Families

**What TN SHIP Actually Does:** TN SHIP is a free counseling service, not a payment program. It helps Medicare beneficiaries understand their options and apply for programs that DO pay for premiums and copays (like Medicare Savings Plans or Extra Help).[2][4]

**Who Qualifies:** Anyone 65+ who is Medicare-eligible, or anyone of any age on Medicare due to disability.[6] No income or asset limits are publicly stated, suggesting the program serves all Medicare beneficiaries regardless of financial status.

**How to Get Started:** Call 1-877-801-0044 (toll-free) or email dda.ship@tn.gov.[4] Your call will route to the correct regional office for your area. Translation services are available.[7]

**What to Expect:** A trained counselor will provide one-on-one assistance reviewing Medicare plan options, help determine eligibility for cost-assistance programs, and answer questions about Medicare Parts A, B, D, Medicare Advantage, Medigap, and related programs.[1][5]

**Critical Difference from Other Programs:** Unlike some Medicare assistance programs, TN SHIP does not directly pay for premiums or services. Instead, it educates beneficiaries and helps them apply for separate programs that provide financial assistance.[2][4] This is the "gotcha" — families expecting direct payment should understand SHIP's role is advisory and application-support.

## Key Distinctions for Your Guide

**This is NOT a cash payment program.** Families often confuse NFCSP with paid caregiver programs. NFCSP provides *services* (respite care, training, counseling) to support unpaid family caregivers, not employment or stipends[2][3].

**The 185% income threshold is a contribution trigger, not a disqualifier.** Families above this threshold can still access services but will be asked to pay based on actual service costs[2].

**Respite care is the guaranteed service.** Up to 6 hours per week is explicitly promised; other services (counseling, training, daycare) depend on what your local Area Agency on Aging and Disability has funded[2][3].

**Functional limitations matter more than diagnosis.** A care recipient must be unable to perform at least 2 ADLs without substantial help, or require supervision due to cognitive/mental impairment — having Alzheimer's alone isn't sufficient[2].

**If your loved one is a veteran**, explore VA programs instead — the Program of Comprehensive Assistance for Family Caregivers (PCAFC) may provide more robust support, including monthly stipends[1][6].

**If your loved one qualifies for Medicaid**, the CHOICES Waiver Program may offer more comprehensive paid caregiver options than NFCSP[6].

To start: **Call 1-866-836-6678** and ask to speak with an Intake Specialist[2][3].

programs.name: Senior Community Service Employment Program (SCSEP)

eligibility.income_limits: Family income at no more than 125% of the federal poverty level. Exact dollar amounts vary annually and by household size; families must contact local providers to verify current thresholds based on HHS Poverty Guidelines (e.g., for 2025, 125% for 1-person household is approximately $19,563; for 2-person $26,397—confirm with provider as not specified in sources).[1][2][4]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned in program sources.
other_requirements: Legal resident of Tennessee
Unemployed
Must live in a county served by a local provider

benefits.value: Part-time community service work experience (e.g., teacher's aides, nurse's aides, library clerks, day care assistance, maintenance) averaging 20 hours per week at minimum wage (federal/state/local highest rate, e.g., $7.25/hour in some areas); includes assessments, skill upgrading, job counseling, job placement assistance; typically lasts about 6 months before transition to unsubsidized employment.[1][2][4][6]

methods: Phone or email regional coordinators (e.g., Jeremiah Muhammad at jmuhammad@greatermemworkforce.com for Shelby/Fayette/Tipton/Lauderdale; Britney Bartholomew at 731-587-4213 ext. 234 or britney.bartholomew@nwthra.org for NW TN; Renae Hawkins at 865-691-2551 ext. 4347 or rhawkins@ethra.org for East TN; statewide info Jeff.Hughes@tn.gov or (731) 989-5111/(800) 372-6013).[1][2][6]
In-person: Schedule appointment at local offices (e.g., 1350 Concourse Ave., Memphis; 124 Weldon Drive, Martin; 9111 Crosspark Drive, Knoxville)
No specific online application or mail mentioned; start by contacting coordinators
application.processing_time: Not specified; involves assessment and placement upon eligibility determination.
application.waitlist: Possible waitlist in some areas; contact local provider to check availability or be placed on list.[6]
forms: No specific form names/numbers listed; intake via coordinator appointment.
documents: Proof of Social Security Number
Identification
Citizenship status
Date of birth
Low income status (e.g., income verification showing ≤125% FPL)

geography.restrictions: Available only in counties served by local grantees (e.g., Shelby/Fayette/Tipton/Lauderdale; NW TN areas; Anderson/Blount/Cocke/Grainger/Hamblen/Jefferson/Loudon/Monroe/Roane/Sevier; openings in several counties statewide—check with provider).[1]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by different local providers (e.g., Greater Memphis Workforce, NWTHRA, ETHRA) with specific counties, contacts, and potential wait times varying by region; pay may reflect local minimum wage.
offices_or_providers: Greater Memphis Workforce: Shelby, Fayette, Tipton, Lauderdale (jmuhammad@greatermemworkforce.com)
NWTHRA: Martin area (731-587-4213 ext. 234)
ETHRA: East TN counties (865-691-2551 ext. 4347)
Statewide inquiries: tn.gov/workforce or Jeff.Hughes@tn.gov

gotchas: Not available statewide—must confirm county coverage with local provider; many areas have limited openings.
Priority enrollment for veterans/qualified spouses, those 65+, low literacy, homelessness risk, low employment prospects, or prior incarceration—others may face waitlists.[2]
Temporary (typically 6 months) part-time subsidized work aimed at unsubsidized job placement, not long-term employment or full benefits.
Income limit is strict at 125% FPL for total family income; no asset test but full verification required.
Must be unemployed at enrollment; focuses on skill-building in community service roles, not guaranteed preferred job type.

## Key Context for Families

**This is not a means-tested program.** Unlike most legal aid, the Tennessee Senior Legal Helpline does not screen by income or assets[7]. If your loved one is 60 or older and lives in Tennessee, they likely qualify.

**Start with the statewide helpline.** Calling 1-844-HELP4TN (1-844-435-7486) is the simplest entry point[7]. Attorneys provide confidential legal advice at no cost, regardless of income[7].

**The program addresses core senior legal crises.** TSLA specifically targets issues that trap seniors in poverty: wrongfully denied benefits, unsafe housing, financial exploitation, and lack of estate planning[1].

**Regional providers vary.** While the statewide helpline is uniform, in-person services depend on which of the four regional legal aid providers serves your county[1]. East Tennessee has a dedicated Legal Assistance for the Elderly Program with its own intake process[3].

**Critical gap in available information:** The search results do not disclose processing times, waitlist status, required documentation, or specific eligibility thresholds beyond age 60. Families should ask these questions directly when calling.

programs.name: Tennessee Long-Term Care Ombudsman Program

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits or financial restrictions apply[3][4][6]
other_requirements: Must be a resident of a certified long-term care facility (nursing homes, assisted living facilities, homes for the aged, adult care homes), or family/friend acting on behalf of such a resident[1][3][4][5][6][7]

benefits.value: Advocacy to identify, investigate, and resolve complaints; provide information on rights, admission processes, long-term services; ensure access to care quality improvements; represent interests before agencies; educate on resident rights including courtesy, private visits, no wrongful transfer/discharge, freedom from abuse[1][3][4][5][6][7]

methods: Statewide toll-free phone: 877-236-0013 (routes to district ombudsman)[4]
Find district ombudsman via https://www.tn.gov/disability-and-aging/disability-aging-programs/long-term-care-ombudsman/district-long-term-care-ombudsman.html[4][8]
Email for volunteer info: teresa.teeple@tn.gov (not primary for complaints)[4]
Regional examples: East TN (ETHRA) phone (865) 691-2551 Ext. 4223 or tkahler@ethra.org[1]; First TN (FTAAAD) phone 423-979-2599[3]
forms: No specific forms mentioned; complaints handled via phone/contact to district ombudsman[4][5]
documents: No documents required; contact directly with concerns[3][4]

geography.restrictions: Services provided through 9 district programs covering 95 counties and ~700 facilities[1][3][4][5][8]
geography.regional_variations: Administered by regional/district ombudsman offices with specific counties served (e.g., ETHRA District 2: Anderson, Blount, etc.; FTAAAD District 1: Carter, Greene, etc.); different contacts and providers per district[1][3][5][8]
offices_or_providers: Districts include First TN (1), East TN (2), Southeast TN (3), Upper Cumberland (4), and others; full list at state site[8]

gotchas: Not a direct service provider (e.g., no healthcare or financial aid)—purely advocacy and complaint resolution[1][4][5][7]
Must contact specific district ombudsman for your county, not statewide directly (use toll-free or district finder)[1][3][4][8]
Families can contact on behalf of residents, but services are facility-resident focused[3][4][7]
Volunteers extend reach but core staff are limited (17.5 FTE across state)[5]
programs.data_shape_notes: no income/asset test; district-based with county-specific offices; advocacy-only, not benefits/services program

programs.name: OPTIONS for Community Living

eligibility.income_limits: No strict income limit; if income exceeds Federal Poverty Guidelines, a cost share is required. (Note: This program is not Medicaid-funded like CHOICES; income affects cost-sharing only.)[4]
other_requirements: Tennessee resident aged 60+ requiring assistance with at least 3 Activities of Daily Living (ADLs) or Instrumental Activities of Daily Living (IADLs), such as mobility, toiletry, hygiene, dressing, light housekeeping, cooking, shopping, bathing, walking without assistance.[2][4][8]
Assistance certified by a medical professional.[2]
For ages 18-60, must be disabled as determined by Social Security (though program targets elderly).[2]

benefits.value: Light housekeeping, assistance with bathing, home delivered meals, case management.[4]

methods: Phone: Call FTAAAD Information and Assistance line at 1-866-836-6678 for screening and application.[2][4]
In-person or mail: Contact local Area Agency on Aging and Disability (AAAD); have intake form ready.[4]
application.processing_time: Intake screening by phone; if eligible and slot available, case manager conducts in-home assessment to set up services. Exact timeline not specified.[4]
application.waitlist: Waiting lists have existed in past years; prioritization based on income and physical need. May qualify but wait for slot.[2]
forms: Intake Form (downloadable from provider websites like ETHRA).[4]
documents: Medical certification of ADL/IADL needs; personal information for intake (have ready to speed process).[2][4]

geography.restrictions: Administered by regional Area Agencies on Aging and Disability (AAADs); not available in all counties.
geography.regional_variations: Services delivered by local AAADs with potential differences in providers, wait times, and availability; e.g., ETHRA covers specific East TN counties (Blount, Claiborne, Campbell, Anderson, Knox, Morgan, Cocke, Grainger, Hamblen, Jefferson, Loudon, Monroe, Roane, Scott, Sevier, Union).[4]
offices_or_providers: Contact statewide intake at 1-866-836-6678 to find local AAAD.[2][4]

programs.name: Tennessee State Health Insurance Assistance Program (TN SHIP) and Senior Medicare Patrol (SMP)

eligibility.income_limits: No income limits; open to anyone with Medicare questions, focused on Medicare-eligible individuals 65+ or under 65 due to disability[2][3][6]
eligibility.asset_limits: No asset limits mentioned; assistance available for low-income programs like Extra Help without SHIP eligibility barriers[3][5]
other_requirements: Medicare beneficiary or family/caregiver
Tennessee resident
No promotion of specific insurance; unbiased service[2][4][6]

benefits.value: Free one-on-one counseling on Medicare enrollment, Part D/Advantage comparisons, eligibility, coverage, rights, appeals; assistance with Medicare Savings Programs/Extra Help applications; fraud prevention/education/reporting via SMP; group presentations, health fairs[2][3][4][5][6]

methods: Phone: 1-877-801-0044 (SHIP statewide toll-free, routes to local); 1-866-836-7677 (SMP statewide)[4][5][6][7]
Email: dda.ship@tn.gov (SHIP); ship@ucdd.org (some regions)[5][6]
Website: www.tnmedicarehelp.com (general); tn.gov/disability-and-aging/disability-aging-programs/tn-ship.html (contact form)[5][6][7]
Local offices via 9 Area Agencies on Aging and Disability (AAAD), e.g., Shelby County: 901-222-4111 or 877-801-0044; Upper Cumberland: 931-432-4150[4][5]
application.processing_time: Immediate phone counseling; plan comparisons may take days (e.g., during Open Enrollment)[6]
forms: Contact form on tn.gov site; plan comparison worksheets for Annual Enrollment (downloadable from regional sites like ucdd.org)[5][6]
documents: Medicare card, current plan details, income info for low-income apps (e.g., Extra Help), but not required for initial counseling[3][5]

geography.restrictions: None; serves all Tennessee via 9 AAADs covering regions (e.g., Mid-South: Shelby/Fayette/Lauderdale/Tipton; Upper Cumberland: 14 counties)[4][5][7]
geography.regional_variations: Delivered through 9 regional AAADs with local counselors/volunteers/phone numbers; same core services but local contacts vary[4][5][7]
offices_or_providers: 9 Area Agencies on Aging and Disability (AAADs) statewide; e.g., Aging Commission of the Mid-South, Upper Cumberland AAAD[4][5][7]

gotchas: Not a healthcare provider—only counseling/education, no direct financial aid or medical services[2][6]
Must disenroll from ACA Marketplace upon Medicare eligibility to avoid penalties[6]
During Open Enrollment (Oct 15-Dec 7), high demand may delay plan comparisons—call Medicare 1-800-MEDICARE for immediate needs[6]
SMP focuses on fraud prevention/reporting, separate but complementary to SHIP[1][4][7]
Volunteers provide much service; training available but not required to receive help[3][6]
programs.data_shape_notes: No income/asset test for core counseling; delivered via 9 regional AAADs with local access points; pairs SHIP counseling with SMP fraud focus; open to all Medicare-related questions
